title: भगवद्गीता अध्याय ३ - कर्मयोग
devanagariText:
॥ अथ तृतीयोऽध्यायः - कर्मयोगः ॥

अर्जुन उवाच —
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन।
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव॥१॥

व्यामिश्रेणेव वाक्येन बुद्धिं मोहयसीव मे।
तदेकं वद निश्चित्य येन श्रेयोऽहमाप्नुयाम्॥२॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
लोकेऽस्मिन्द्विविधा निष्ठा पुरा प्रोक्ता मयानघ।
ज्ञानयोगेन साङ्ख्यानां कर्मयोगेन योगिनाम्॥३॥

न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्नुते।
न च सन्न्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति॥४॥

न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत्।
कार्यते ह्यवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः॥५॥

कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य य आस्ते मनसा स्मरन्।
इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः स उच्यते॥६॥

यस्त्विन्द्रियाणि मनसा नियम्यारभतेऽर्जुन।
कर्मेन्द्रियैः कर्मयोगमसक्तः स विशिष्यते॥७॥

नियतं कुरु कर्म त्वं कर्म ज्यायो ह्यकर्मणः।
शरीरयात्रापि च ते न प्रसिद्ध्येदकर्मणः॥८॥

यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं कर्मबन्धनः।
तदर्थं कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर॥९॥

सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा पुरोवाच प्रजापतिः।
अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वमेष वोऽस्त्विष्टकामधुक्॥१०॥

देवान्भावयतानेन ते देवा भावयन्तु वः।
परस्परं भावयन्तः श्रेयः परमवाप्स्यथ॥११॥

इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः।
तैर्दत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः॥१२॥

यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तो मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः।
भुञ्जते ते त्वघं पापा ये पचन्त्यात्मकारणात्॥१३॥

अन्नाद्भवन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः।
यज्ञाद्भवति पर्जन्यो यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भवः॥१४॥

कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवं विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भवम्।
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम्॥१५॥

एवं प्रवर्तितं चक्रं नानुवर्तयतीह यः।
अघायुरिन्द्रियारामो मोघं पार्थ स जीवति॥१६॥

यस्त्वात्मरतिरेव स्यादात्मतृप्तश्च मानवः।
आत्मन्येव च सन्तुष्टस्तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते॥१७॥

नैव तस्य कृतेनार्थो नाकृतेनेह कश्चन।
न चास्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः॥१८॥

तस्मादसक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर।
असक्तो ह्याचरन्कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः॥१९॥

कर्मणैव हि संसिद्धिमास्थिता जनकादयः।
लोकसङ्ग्रहमेवापि सम्पश्यन्कर्तुमर्हसि॥२०॥

यद्यदाचरति श्रेष्ठस्तत्तदेवेतरो जनः।
स यत्प्रमाणं कुरुते लोकस्तदनुवर्तते॥२१॥

न मे पार्थास्ति कर्तव्यं त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन।
नानवाप्तमवाप्तव्यं वर्त एव च कर्मणि॥२२॥

यदि ह्यहं न वर्तेयं जातु कर्मण्यतन्द्रितः।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः॥२३॥

उत्सीदेयुरिमे लोका न कुर्यां कर्म चेदहम्।
सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्यामुपहन्यामिमाः प्रजाः॥२४॥

सक्ताः कर्मण्यविद्वांसो यथा कुर्वन्ति भारत।
कुर्याद्विद्वांस्तथासक्तश्चिकीर्षुर्लोकसङ्ग्रहम्॥२५॥

प्रकृतेः क्रियमाणानि गुणैः कर्माणि सर्वशः।
अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा कर्ताहमिति मन्यते॥२७॥

श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः परधर्मात्स्वनुष्ठितात्।
स्वधर्मे निधनं श्रेयः परधर्मो भयावहः॥३५॥

अर्जुन उवाच —
अथ केन प्रयुक्तोऽयं पापं चरति पूरुषः।
अनिच्छन्नपि वार्ष्णेय बलादिव नियोजितः॥३६॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
काम एष क्रोध एष रजोगुणसमुद्भवः।
महाशनो महापाप्मा विद्ध्येनमिह वैरिणम्॥३७॥

धूमेनाव्रियते वह्निर्यथादर्शो मलेन च।
यथोल्बेनावृतो गर्भस्तथा तेनेदमावृतम्॥३८॥

आवृतं ज्ञानमेतेन ज्ञानिनो नित्यवैरिणा।
कामरूपेण कौन्तेय दुष्पूरेणानलेन च॥३९॥

इन्द्रियाणि मनो बुद्धिरस्याधिष्ठानमुच्यते।
एतैर्विमोहयत्येष ज्ञानमावृत्य देहिनम्॥४०॥

तस्मात्त्वमिन्द्रियाण्यादौ नियम्य भरतर्षभ।
पाप्मानं प्रजहि ह्येनं ज्ञानविज्ञाननाशनम्॥४१॥

इन्द्रियाणि पराण्याहुरिन्द्रियेभ्यः परं मनः।
मनसस्तु परा बुद्धिर्यो बुद्धेः परतस्तु सः॥४२॥

एवं बुद्धेः परं बुद्ध्वा संस्तभ्यात्मानमात्मना।
जहि शत्रुं महाबाहो कामरूपं दुरासदम्॥४३॥

॥ इति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे
श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे कर्मयोगो नाम तृतीयोऽध्यायः ॥
transliteration:
|| Atha Tritiyo'dhyayah - Karma Yogah ||

Arjuna Uvacha —
Jyayasi Chet-Karmanas-Te Mata Buddhir-Janardana.
Tat-Kim Karmani Ghore Mam Niyojayasi Keshava.||1||

Vyamishreneva Vakyena Buddhim Mohayasi-Iva Me.
Tad-Ekam Vada Nishchitya Yena Shreyo'ham-Apnuyam.||2||

Shri Bhagavan Uvacha —
Loke'smin Dvividha Nishtha Pura Prokta Maya-Anagha.
Jnana-Yogena Sankhyanam Karma-Yogena Yoginam.||3||

Na Karmanam-Anarambhan-Naishkarmyam Purusho'shnute.
Na Cha Sannyasanad-Eva Siddhim Samadhigachchhati.||4||

Na Hi Kashchit-Kshanam-Api Jatu Tishthaty-Akarmakrit.
Karyate Hy-Avashah Karma Sarvah Prakriti-Jair-Gunaih.||5||

Karma-Indriyani Samyamya Ya Aste Manasa Smaran.
Indriya-Arthan Vimudha-Atma Mithya-Acharah Sa Uchyate.||6||

Yas-Tv-Indriyani Manasa Niyamya-Arabhate'rjuna.
Karma-Indriyaih Karma-Yogam-Asaktah Sa Vishishyate.||7||

Niyatam Kuru Karma Tvam Karma Jyayo Hy-Akarmanah.
Sharira-Yatra-Api Cha Te Na Prasiddhyed-Akarmanah.||8||

Yajna-Arthat Karmano'nyatra Loko'yam Karma-Bandhanah.
Tad-Artham Karma Kaunteya Mukta-Sangah Samachara.||9||

Saha-Yajnah Prajah Srishtva Puro-Vacha Prajapatih.
Anena Prasavishyadhvam-Esha Vo'stv-Ishta-Kamadhuk.||10||

Devan Bhavayata-Anena Te Deva Bhavantu Vah.
Parasparam Bhavayantah Shreyah Param-Avapsyatha.||11||

Ishtan Bhogan Hi Vo Deva Dasyante Yajna-Bhavitah.
Tair-Dattan-Apradaya-Ibhyo Yo Bhunkte Stena Eva Sah.||12||

Yajna-Shishta-Ashinah Santo Muchyante Sarva-Kilbishaih.
Bhunjate Te Tv-Agham Papa Ye Pachanty-Atma-Karanat.||13||

Annad-Bhavanti Bhutani Parjanyad-Anna-Sambhavah.
Yajnad-Bhavati Parjanyo Yajnah Karma-Samudbhavah.||14||

Karma Brahmo-Udbhavam Viddhi Brahma-Akshara-Samudbhavam.
Tasmat Sarva-Gatam Brahma Nityam Yajne Pratishthitam.||15||

Evam Pravartitam Chakram Na-Anuvartayati-Iha Yah.
Agha-Ayur-Indriya-Aramo Mogham Partha Sa Jivati.||16||

Yas-Tv-Atma-Ratir-Eva Syad-Atma-Triptash-Cha Manavah.
Atmany-Eva Cha Santushtas-Tasya Karyam Na Vidyate.||17||

Naiva Tasya Kritena-Artho Na-Akriteneha Kashchana.
Na Cha-Asya Sarva-Bhuteshu Kashchid-Artha-Vyapashrayah.||18||

Tasmad-Asaktah Satatam Karyam Karma Samachara.
Asakto Hy-Acharan Karma Param-Apnoti Purushah.||19||

Karmanai-Va Hi Samsiddhim-Asthita Janaka-Adayah.
Loka-Sangraham-Eva-Api Sampashyan Kartum-Arhasi.||20||

Yad-Yad-Acharati Shreshthah Tat-Tad-Evetaro Janah.
Sa Yat-Pramanam Kurute Lokas-Tad-Anuvartate.||21||

Na Me Partha-Asti Kartavyam Trishu Lokeshu Kinchana.
Na-Anavaptam-Avaptavyam Varta Eva Cha Karmani.||22||

Shreyaan Sva-Dharmo Vigunah Para-Dharmat Sv-Anushthitat.
Sva-Dharme Nidhanam Shreyah Para-Dharmo Bhaya-Avahah.||35||

Kama Esha Krodha Esha Rajo-Guna-Samudbhavah.
Maha-Ashano Maha-Papma Viddhy-Enam-Iha Vairinam.||37||

Evam Buddheh Param Buddhva Samstabhya-Atmanam-Atmana.
Jahi Shatrum Maha-Baho Kama-Rupam Durasadam.||43||

|| Iti Shrimad-Bhagavad-Gitasu-Upanishatsu Brahma-Vidyayam Yoga-Shastre
Shri-Krishna-Arjuna-Samvade Karma-Yogo Nama Tritiyo'dhyayah ||
hindiMeaning: तृतीय अध्याय 'कर्मयोग' में श्रीकृष्ण निष्काम कर्म का महत्व समझाते हैं। अर्जुन पूछते हैं कि यदि बुद्धि कर्म से श्रेष्ठ है तो युद्ध क्यों? कृष्ण कहते हैं कि कोई भी एक क्षण भी कर्म किए बिना नहीं रह सकता — प्रकृति के गुण सबको कर्म में प्रवृत्त करते हैं। 'नियतं कुरु कर्म त्वं' — अपना नियत कर्म करो, क्योंकि कर्म न करने से शरीर-यात्रा भी नहीं चलती। यज्ञ-चक्र (कर्म-प्रकृति-परमात्मा) का वर्णन है। कृष्ण स्वयं का उदाहरण देते हैं — मुझे तीनों लोकों में कोई कर्तव्य नहीं, फिर भी मैं कर्म करता हूँ। 'श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः' — अपना धर्म अपूर्ण भी परधर्म से श्रेष्ठ है। अंत में काम और क्रोध को सबसे बड़ा शत्रु बताकर उन्हें जीतने का उपदेश दिया।
benefits: Inspires selfless action without attachment to results
Teaches the importance of performing one's duty (Svadharma)
Explains the cosmic cycle of Yajna and its role in creation
Guides on controlling desire and anger — the greatest enemies
Develops leadership qualities through righteous example
description: The Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 - Karma Yoga, known in Sanskrit as भगवद्गीता अध्याय ३ - कर्मयोग, is a sacred prayer in praise of Lord Krishna. Rooted in Bhagavad Gita - Mahabharata, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Devotees recite this prayer to inspire selfless action without attachment to results and teach the importance of performing one's duty (Svadharma). It is also said to develop leadership qualities through righteous example. This sacred text is ideally recited on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami for enhanced blessings. At 43 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 20 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 - Karma Yoga in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 Karma Yoga in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. 43 verses on selfless action, duty, and conquering desire.

seoFaqs.question: What is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 — Karma Yoga?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 (भगवद्गीता अध्याय ३), titled Karma Yoga (the Yoga of Action), is the third chapter of the Srimad Bhagavad Gita and contains 43 verses. It is the definitive exposition of the path of selfless action introduced in Chapter 2. Arjuna opens with a confused question: if buddhi (knowledge) is superior to karma (action), why are you urging me to terrible action? (3.1-2). Krishna replies that there are two paths — jnana-yoga for the contemplative Sankhyas and karma-yoga for the active yogis — but neither can be skipped. Action is unavoidable for embodied beings; even sustaining the body requires it. The path forward is therefore not abandonment of action but its transformation through yajna (sacrifice) — performing all action as an offering to the Divine. The chapter exalts King Janaka and Lord Krishna himself as supreme examples of karma-yogis. (Source: Srimad Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3; Mahabharata Bhishma Parva.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is action necessary according to Chapter 3?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 3.4-3.8 establish the necessity of action: (1) Verse 3.4: Na karmanam-anarambhan-naishkarmyam purusho'shnute, na cha sannyasanad-eva siddhim samadhigachchhati — One does not attain freedom from action by abstaining from action, nor does one attain perfection by mere renunciation. (2) Verse 3.5: Na hi kashchit-kshanam-api jatu tishthaty-akarma-krit, karyate hy-avashah karma sarvah prakriti-jair-gunaih — No one can remain even for a moment without doing action; everyone is compelled to act helplessly by the gunas born of prakriti. (3) Verse 3.6: One who restrains the karmic organs but mentally dwells on objects is a hypocrite (mithyachara). (4) Verse 3.7: But one who controls the senses with the mind and engages the karmic organs in karma-yoga without attachment — that one excels. (5) Verse 3.8: Niyatam kuru karma tvam karma jyayo hy-akarmanah, sharira-yatra-pi cha te na prasiddhyed-akarmanah — Perform your prescribed duty, for action is superior to inaction; without action even the maintenance of your body would not be possible. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 3.4-3.8.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the cosmic wheel of yajna (sacrifice) taught in Chapter 3?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 3.10-3.16 present the foundational cosmology of yajna-chakra (the cosmic wheel of sacrifice): At creation Prajapati produced humans together with yajna and said: by this you shall multiply; let this be your milch-cow that yields all desires. By yajna nourish the gods, and may the gods nourish you; thus mutually nourishing each other you shall attain the highest good. Nourished by yajna the gods will give you desired enjoyments; one who enjoys these gifts without offering anything to them in return is verily a thief. The good who eat the remnants of yajna are released from all sins; those who cook only for themselves verily eat sin. From food beings come into being; from rain food is produced; from yajna rain comes; yajna is born of action; action springs from the Veda; the Veda from the Imperishable Brahman — therefore the all-pervading Brahman is ever established in yajna. One who does not follow this revolving wheel here is sinful, sense-indulgent, and lives in vain. This yajna-chakra cosmology is the foundation of all Hindu ritual and ethical life. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 3.10-3.16.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Chapter 3 say about Loka-sangraha (welfare of the world)?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 3.20-3.26 give the celebrated teaching of loka-sangraha (the gathering-together / welfare of the world). Verse 3.20: Karmanaiva hi samsiddhim asthita Janakadayah, loka-sangraham-evapi sampashyan-kartum-arhasi — Through action alone King Janaka and others attained perfection; you should perform action for the holding-together of the world. Verse 3.21: Yad-yad-acharati shreshthah tat-tad-eva-itaro janah, sa yat-pramanam kurute lokas-tad-anuvartate — Whatever a great person does, the lesser ones imitate; whatever standard he sets, the people follow. Verse 3.22: Krishna himself declares: I have nothing to attain in the three worlds, nor anything ungained that needs to be attained; yet I engage in action — for if I did not, men would follow my path and the world would perish (3.23-24). The wise should act unattached for loka-sangraha just as the unwise act with attachment (3.25). The leader-as-example principle that Krishna teaches here is the foundation of all dharmic leadership. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 3.20-3.26.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous verse Shreyan-svadharmo vigunah in Chapter 3?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 3.35 contains one of the most quoted ethical teachings of the Gita: Shreyan-svadharmo vigunah para-dharmat sv-anushthitat, svadharme nidhanam shreyah para-dharmo bhayavahah — Better is one's own dharma, even if imperfect, than another's dharma well-performed; better is death in one's own dharma; another's dharma is fraught with fear. The teaching of svadharma — that each person has a duty arising from their own nature, situation and stage of life, and that following this even imperfectly is superior to performing another's duty perfectly — is one of the most distinctive contributions of the Gita to ethical thought. The verse is quoted again in Chapter 18.47 in slightly different form. It validates the diversity of vocations, the legitimacy of different paths in life, and warns against borrowed lives lived according to others' patterns. The verse is the ethical foundation of varna-svabhava and authentic individual calling. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 3.35; cf. 18.47.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Chapter 3 teach about kama (desire) as the root enemy?
seoFaqs.answer: The chapter closes (verses 3.36-3.43) with Arjuna's piercing question: Atha kena prayukto'yam papam charati purushah, anichchhann-api Varshneya balad-iva niyojitah — But by what is one impelled to commit sin, even unwillingly, as if compelled by force? Krishna's reply (3.37): Kama esha krodha esha rajo-guna-samudbhavah, mahashano maha-papma viddhy-enam-iha vairinam — It is desire (kama), it is anger (krodha), born of rajas — all-devouring, the great sinner; know this to be the enemy here on earth. He compares it to fire covered by smoke, a mirror by dust, an embryo by the womb (3.38). Verse 3.42 gives the famous hierarchy: Indriyani parany-ahur indriyebhyah param manah, manasas-tu para buddhir yo buddheh paratas-tu sah — They say the senses are higher (than the body); higher than the senses is the mind; higher than the mind is the intellect; and what is higher than the intellect is the Self. Therefore knowing the Self as higher than the intellect, restrain the self by the Self and slay this enemy in the form of desire (3.43). (Source: Bhagavad Gita 3.36-3.43.)

seoFaqs.question: When is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Chapter 3 is recited on Day 3 of an 18-day Gita Parayana cycle. It is especially recited by karma-yogis, householders, professionals and leaders — anyone whose svadharma involves engagement with the world rather than withdrawal from it. Specific occasions include: Wednesday (Budhvar — Krishna's day), Ekadashi, Gita Jayanti (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi), Janmashtami, the start of any new project or business venture (the loka-sangraha verses 3.20-26 are particularly auspicious for leaders), and as a daily morning karma-yoga meditation. The chapter is also chanted for: clarity in performing duty without attachment, victory over inner enemies of kama-krodha (verses 3.36-43), inspiration from the example of King Janaka (the karma-yogi king), and to overcome confusion between the path of action and the path of renunciation. (Sources: Gita Mahatmya; classical Karma Yoga sadhana tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Chapter 3 connect to the verse-by-verse Gita pages on this site?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 — Karma Yoga contains 43 verses, each studied verse-by-verse on this site at /gita/chapter-3/verse-1 through /gita/chapter-3/verse-43. The verse-level pages provide the Devanagari, transliteration, word-by-word meaning, English translation, Hindi anvaya and commentary by classical acharyas (Shankara, Ramanuja, Madhva, Gyaneshwar) for each shloka — particularly important for the necessity-of-action verses (3.4-9), the yajna-chakra cosmology (3.10-16), the loka-sangraha verses (3.20-26), the celebrated svadharma verse (3.35), and the kama-as-enemy verses (3.36-43). This stotra page presents the entire chapter as a single text for parayana — the supreme practical manual for engaged spiritual life. The chapter pairs naturally with Chapter 5 (Karma Sannyasa Yoga), Chapter 6 (Dhyana Yoga), and Chapter 18 (Moksha Sannyasa Yoga) which together expound the complete path of action-renunciation-meditation. (Source: Stotra and Gita parayana traditions.)
